title: Brioche — classic Parisian
subtitle: Enriched French bread with eggs and cold butter
excerpt: A slow-mixed, high-butter enriched dough with a feathery crumb and a deep golden crust. Cold butter is worked in gradually after the gluten has developed, then the dough cold-proofs overnight. Rich enough to eat plain, but the classic use is toasted for breakfast.
sourceNotes: Classic Parisian brioche is documented in Urbain Dubois and Emile Bernard, La Cuisine Classique (1856), and in Auguste Escoffier, Le Guide Culinaire (1903), which gives the proportions of butter-to-flour (from one third to an equal weight of butter) as the defining variable of quality. The cold-butter incorporation method (beurrage) described here follows the Escoffier tradition and the standard boulangerie technique still taught in French pastry schools. No single public-domain text can be cited as the single source; the recipe draws across the classical French tradition.

recipe.freezeNotes: Slice cool; freeze flat in a sealed bag for up to 2 months. Toast from frozen.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: After shaping, refrigerate overnight (up to 14 hours). Bake straight from the fridge with an extra 5 minutes.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional oven. Fan at 170°C. The enriched dough browns quickly; watch the colour after 20 minutes.

glossaryTerms.definition: The technique of incorporating cold cubed butter into a developed dough after full gluten development. The butter is added in stages at low speed so the fat coats the gluten strands without tearing them. A correctly beurraged brioche dough is smooth, pulls cleanly from the bowl, and feels cool and slightly tacky.

content.text: Brioche works because the gluten develops before the fat arrives. If you add the butter too early, the fat coats the flour and the gluten never builds. The sequence of flour, eggs, and yeast first, with cold butter only after the dough has passed the windowpane test, is what separates brioche from a greasy enriched loaf. The process called
takes patience: butter goes in a few cubes at a time over ten to fifteen minutes. When it is done, the dough is silky, elastic, and holds its shape without springing back aggressively.

Put the flour, yeast, sugar, and salt into the bowl of a stand mixer fitted with the dough hook. Keep the yeast and salt on opposite sides of the bowl. Add the eggs and warm milk. Mix on low speed for 2 minutes to combine, then increase to medium and mix for 10 minutes until the dough is smooth and passes a windowpane test: stretch a small piece between your fingers until it is thin enough to see light through without tearing.

With the mixer on medium-low, add the cold butter a few cubes at a time, waiting for each addition to be fully incorporated before adding the next. This takes 12 to 15 minutes. Do not rush it. Once all the butter is in, increase to medium and mix for a further 5 minutes until the dough is smooth, glossy, and pulls cleanly from the sides of the bowl.

Transfer to a lightly oiled bowl, cover with cling film, and leave to rise at room temperature for 90 minutes until noticeably puffy. Knock back, re-cover, and refrigerate for at least 8 hours or overnight. Cold dough is much easier to shape.

Tip the cold dough onto a lightly floured surface. Shape into a log and press into a buttered 900 g loaf tin. Cover loosely and leave to prove at room temperature for 1 hour until the dough domes above the tin rim. Brush the surface with beaten egg. Bake at 190°C conventional (170°C fan) for 28 to 32 minutes until deep mahogany. The internal temperature should reach 93°C. Leave in the tin for 10 minutes, then cool on a rack for at least 30 minutes before slicing.
